hook.scenario: Flipkart shows '20% off, then an extra 10% off'. Your friend says 'nice, 30% off'.
hook.question: Is it actually 30% off? And does it matter which discount comes first?

mental_model.intuition: A percentage is a multiplier, not an amount.
mental_model.description: +20% means x1.2, -20% means x0.8. Once every % is a multiplier, a chain of changes (discounts, interest, growth, depreciation) collapses to one multiplication - and you stop adding them.

pattern_discovery.setup: Apply two discounts back to back and watch the final price.
cases: Rs100, 20% off then 10% off -> Rs72 (not Rs70)
Rs100, 50% off then 50% off -> Rs25 (not Rs0)
pattern_discovery.prompt: Why is it never the simple sum? What is the second discount taken OF?
pattern_discovery.rule: Each discount applies to what's LEFT, not the original - so changes MULTIPLY: 0.8 x 0.9 = 0.72, i.e. 28% off, never 30%. Order doesn't matter because multiplication commutes.

formula.statement: Final = Original x product of (1 +/- r_i). Successive a% then b% gives net = a + b + ab/100.
formula.explain: The single idea behind discounts, simple/compound interest, growth and depreciation: a % change is a multiplier; chain them by multiplying, not adding.

shortcuts.title: 10% is your base unit
shortcuts.trick: Find 10% (just move the decimal), then build any percentage from it.

shortcuts.title: Successive % in one step
shortcuts.trick: a% then b% gives net = a + b + ab/100 (signs included).

common_mistakes.title: Adding successive percentages
common_mistakes.explanation: 20% then 10% is NOT 30% off - the second % is taken of the already-reduced amount, so it's 28%.

common_mistakes.title: Percentage of the wrong base
common_mistakes.explanation: Profit% is on COST price, discount% is on MARKED price. Same number, different whole - mixing the base is the #1 error.

recall_questions.q: Is a 20% then 10% discount the same as 30% off?
recall_questions.answer: No - it's 28% off (x0.8 x 0.9 = 0.72). Successive percentages multiply, they don't add.

recall_questions.q: Does the order of two successive discounts change the final price?
Fast mental math: 35% of 260 using the 10% base unit.

oa_questions.question: A shopkeeper marks up his goods 20% above cost, then offers a 20% discount. Profit or loss %?
oa_questions.approach: x1.2 x 0.8 = 0.96, i.e. 4% below cost. The markup base (cost) and the discount base (marked price) differ, so the two 20%s never cancel.

oa_questions.question: A salary rises 10% one year and falls 10% the next. Net change over two years?
oa_questions.approach: x1.1 x 0.9 = 0.99 -> down 1%. A rise then equal fall always loses a little (the fall is on a bigger base).
